content:
# Leviticus Chapter 18 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68v–69r
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 104–105
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_018.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 18 opens the Holiness Code (chapters 18–27), extending the concern for holiness from the sanctuary into every domain of communal life. The chapter is constructed with deliberate literary architecture: an opening frame (vv. 1–5) invoking the Egypt-Canaan contrast and the life-promise of va-chai bahem; a central catalogue of prohibited sexual unions (vv. 6–23) organized by degree of kinship and type of transgression; and a closing frame (vv. 24–30) grounding the prohibitions in the moral ecology of the land itself. The governing idiom throughout is le-gallot ervah — "to uncover nakedness" — which functions as the chapter's consistent legal euphemism for prohibited sexual intercourse. This idiom appears approximately twenty-four times in the chapter and is preserved intact throughout the consensus, per the CRITICAL RULING.

Three verses require special attention. Verse 5 (va-chai bahem, "he shall live by them") is among the most theologically weighty phrases in the entire Torah, cited by Ezekiel, Nehemiah, and Paul. Verse 21 (the Molech prohibition) sits within the sexual catalogue for thematic reasons: the misuse of generative power reaches its extreme in the destruction of the life that sexuality produces. Verses 22 and 23 employ two distinct evaluative terms — to'evah ("abomination") and tevel ("depravity") — which are rendered distinctly throughout.

The closing frame (vv. 24–30) employs one of the most striking metaphors in the Pentateuch: va-taqi ha-aretz et-yoshveiha, "the land vomited out its inhabitants." The verb qi (to vomit) is preserved viscerally, as the MT intends. The same moral standard is applied to Israel (v. 28): if the land vomited the Canaanites, it will vomit Israel too. The LXX softens this to "was disgusted with" (προσώχθισεν) — the consensus follows the MT's forceful image.

Key LXX variant noted: at v. 21, Vaticanus reads ἄρχοντι ("to the ruler") rather than Μολόχ, likely reflecting an early Jewish avoidance of the divine name; at v. 25, the LXX softens the vomiting metaphor; at v. 23, tevel is rendered παράνομον ("unlawful") rather than a depravity-term. These variants are noted but the consensus follows the MT.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | ervah = "nakedness" | le-gallot ervah = "uncover nakedness" (idiom preserved throughout — not resolved to "have sexual relations") | she'er besaro = "close relative" | teva'ah = "abomination" | zimmah = "depravity" (v. 17) | tevel = "depravity" (v. 23) | Molekh = "Molech" (proper name preserved) | va-chai bahem = "he shall live by them" | chuqqah = "statute/statutes" | mishpat = "rule/rules" | tame = "unclean/make unclean" | va-taqi = "vomited out"

---

## Consensus Translation

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: I am the LORD your God.

**3** You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived, and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan, to which I am bringing you. You shall not walk in their statutes.

**4** You shall observe my rules and keep my statutes and walk in them. I am the LORD your God.

**5** You shall keep my statutes and my rules; the person who does them shall live by them. I am the LORD.

**6** "None of you shall approach any of his close relatives to uncover nakedness. I am the LORD.

**7** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father or the nakedness of your mother; she is your mother, you shall not uncover her nakedness.

**8** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife; it is your father's nakedness.

**9** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your sister — the daughter of your father or the daughter of your mother — whether born at home or born outside; you shall not uncover her nakedness.

**10** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your son's daughter or your daughter's daughter, for their nakedness is your own nakedness.

**11** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife's daughter, fathered by your father; she is your sister, you shall not uncover her nakedness.

**12** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's sister; she is your father's close relative.

**13** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister, for she is your mother's close relative.

**14** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's brother; you shall not approach his wife — she is your aunt.

**15** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your daughter-in-law; she is your son's wife, you shall not uncover her nakedness.

**16** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your brother's wife; it is your brother's nakedness.

**17** You shall not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter, and you shall not take her son's daughter or her daughter's daughter to uncover her nakedness; they are close relatives — it is depravity.

**18** And you shall not take a woman as a rival to her sister, to uncover her nakedness while her sister is still alive.

**19** You shall not approach a woman to uncover her nakedness during her menstrual impurity.

**20** And you shall not lie carnally with your neighbor's wife, making yourself unclean with her.

**21** You shall not give any of your children to pass them through to Molech, and so profane the name of your God. I am the LORD.

**22** You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination.

**23** And you shall not give your emission to any animal, making yourself unclean with it; nor shall a woman offer herself to an animal to lie with it — it is depravity.

**24** "Do not make yourselves unclean by any of these things, for by all these the nations I am driving out before you have made themselves unclean,

**25** and the land became unclean, so that I visited its iniquity upon it, and the land vomited out its inhabitants.

**26** But you shall keep my statutes and my rules and do none of these abominations — neither the native nor the sojourner who sojourns among you —

**27** for all these abominations the people of the land did who were before you, and the land became unclean —

**28** lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean, as it vomited out the nation that was before you.

**29** For everyone who does any of these abominations — those persons shall be cut off from among their people.

**30** So keep my charge never to practice any of these abominable customs that were practiced before you, and do not make yourselves unclean by them. I am the LORD your God."

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula.** EMET: "And the LORD spoke to Moshe." LOGOS: "And the LORD spoke to Moses." PNEUMA: "The LORD spoke to Moses." Consensus: "And the LORD spoke to Moses" — "Moses" is the standard English name form throughout the project. EMET's "Moshe" is set aside per the established convention (see Gen/Exod series). PNEUMA drops the opening conjunction "And" (waw-consecutive vayedabber); the consensus retains it as it marks narrative continuity from ch. 17. This is also consistent with the Holiness Code's stylistic use of the vayedabber formula.

**V. 2 — Opening speech formula.** EMET: "Speak to the sons of Yisra'el and you shall say to them." LOGOS: "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them." PNEUMA: "Speak to the Israelites and tell them." Consensus: "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them" — LOGOS's rendering is standard. "Israelites" (PNEUMA) is shorthand that loses the covenant genealogical resonance of benei yisrael; "sons of Israel" is the ruling form. "Tell them" (PNEUMA) is too casual for the speech-act formula; "say to them" is the legal-text standard.

**V. 3 — "As they do" vs. "as the doings of."** EMET: "As the doings of the land of Egypt... you shall not do." LOGOS: "You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt." PNEUMA: "You must not live by the customs of Egypt." Consensus: follows LOGOS's form — "You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt... and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan." EMET's "doings of the land" is more literal (ma'aseh eretz) but the LOGOS rendering is idiomatic while accurate. PNEUMA's "customs" for ma'aseh is a significant departure — the Hebrew word is "doings/deeds," not "customs" (which would be minhagim); "customs" flattens the moral evaluation inherent in ma'aseh (deeds with moral weight). "Where you lived" (LOGOS) vs. "in which you dwelt" (EMET) vs. "where you used to live" (PNEUMA): consensus adopts "where you lived" (LOGOS) — simple, accurate, and idiomatic.

**V. 4 — "Rules" vs. "judgments" vs. "ordinances."** EMET: "My judgments you shall do and my statutes you shall keep." LOGOS: "You shall observe my rules and keep my statutes." PNEUMA: "Follow my ordinances and keep my statutes." Consensus: "You shall observe my rules and keep my statutes" (LOGOS). The Hebrew pair is mishpatim ("judgments/rules") and chuqqot ("statutes"). "Rules" (LOGOS, ESV) is preferred over "judgments" (EMET — too judicial in English connotation) and "ordinances" (PNEUMA — too ecclesiastical). "Observe" (LOGOS) vs. "do" (EMET) vs. "follow" (PNEUMA): "observe" captures both doing and watching/keeping; "do" is literal but bare; consensus adopts "observe." The order in the Hebrew places mishpatim before chuqqot (observe rules, keep statutes).

**V. 5 — va-chai bahem / "he shall live by them."** EMET: "which a man shall do — and he shall live by them." LOGOS: "if a person does them, he shall live by them." PNEUMA: "the person who does them will live by them." Consensus: "the person who does them shall live by them." All three preserve the critical idiom va-chai bahem. "Shall live" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over "will live" (PNEUMA) — the volitional/promissory force of the Qal perfect is better rendered with "shall." "Person" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is preferred over "man" (EMET) — the Hebrew adam is gender-inclusive here, denoting any human being. "A man shall do" (EMET) introduces unnecessary gender specificity. The full verse is restructured slightly for English flow.

**V. 6 — "close relatives" vs. "close flesh" vs. "close blood relative."** EMET: "to any of his close flesh." LOGOS: "any one of his close relatives." PNEUMA: "a close blood relative." Consensus: "any of his close relatives" — she'er besaro is rendered "close relative" throughout (LOGOS's established term). EMET's "close flesh" is a valid literal rendering (she'er = "flesh/close one") but awkward in English. PNEUMA's "close blood relative" adds "blood," which is interpretive — the Hebrew does not specify blood vs. marriage here; the list itself defines the scope. "None of you shall approach" (LOGOS) vs. "Any man — to any of his close flesh you shall not approach" (EMET) vs. "No one is to approach" (PNEUMA): consensus follows LOGOS's "None of you shall approach" — smoother, and the direct address plural is implied in the Hebrew lo tiqrevu.

**V. 7 — Father's nakedness / mother's nakedness.** All three preserve the double formulation. EMET: "The nakedness of your father and the nakedness of your mother you shall not uncover." LOGOS: "You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father or the nakedness of your mother." PNEUMA: "Do not dishonor your father by having relations with your mother." Consensus: follows LOGOS's word order (prohibition stated first, then the two objects). PNEUMA's substitution of "dishonor your father by having relations with your mother" for "uncover the nakedness of your father or the nakedness of your mother" is a serious departure: it (a) resolves the "uncover nakedness" idiom against the CRITICAL RULING, (b) replaces "the nakedness of your mother" with the father's dishonor as the primary concern, and (c) omits the second clause entirely. The consensus preserves both "nakedness of your father" and "nakedness of your mother" and the full idiomatic expression. "She is your mother, you shall not uncover her nakedness" — all three preserve this closing clause; consensus adopts it.

**V. 8 — Father's wife.** EMET: "The nakedness of your father's wife you shall not uncover — it is your father's nakedness." LOGOS: same structure. PNEUMA: "Do not have relations with your father's wife. That would dishonor your father." Consensus: "You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife; it is your father's nakedness" — the idiom is preserved. PNEUMA's substitution of "dishonor" for the nakedness-idiom is set aside per CRITICAL RULING.

**V. 9 — Sister prohibition / "born outside."** EMET: "born outside" (lit. for moledet chuts). LOGOS: "born abroad." PNEUMA: "born elsewhere." Consensus: "born outside" — this is the most literal rendering of chuts ("outside, without, abroad"). "Born abroad" (LOGOS) could imply a foreign country; the Hebrew means simply "outside the household." "Born at home or born outside" captures the domestic/non-domestic distinction. EMET: "you shall not uncover their nakedness" (plural suffix). LOGOS: same. PNEUMA: omits the closing prohibition entirely. Consensus: retains the closing "you shall not uncover her nakedness" (singular, following the MT's shift to singular in the final clause) — this is the standard form for the verse-closing prohibition throughout the catalogue.

**V. 10 — Granddaughter / "your own nakedness."** EMET: "for their nakedness." LOGOS: "for their nakedness is your own nakedness." PNEUMA: "That would dishonor yourself." Consensus: follows LOGOS — "for their nakedness is your own nakedness." The MT adds ki ervatekha hennah ("for their nakedness is yours") — this is explicitly present in the Hebrew and is theologically important (the granddaughter is of the grandfather's own flesh, so to violate her is self-violation). EMET's translation appears truncated at this point. PNEUMA's "dishonor yourself" replaces the nakedness formulation entirely.

**V. 11 — Half-sister by father's wife.** EMET: "begotten of your father" (for moledet avikha). LOGOS: "fathered by your father." PNEUMA: omits this clause. Consensus: "fathered by your father" (LOGOS) — "fathered" is the project's standard rendering for the male-subject yalad (established in Genesis series). EMET's "begotten" is acceptable but "fathered" is the ruling term.

**V. 12 — Father's sister / "close relative."** EMET: "she is the near-kin of your father." LOGOS: "she is your father's close relative." PNEUMA: "She is your father's own flesh." Consensus: "she is your father's close relative" (LOGOS) — she'er avikha = "your father's flesh/close-kin." "Close relative" is the established rendering for she'er throughout the chapter.

**V. 13 — Mother's sister.** EMET: "she is the near-kin of your mother." LOGOS: "she is your mother's close relative." PNEUMA: "She is your mother's own flesh." Consensus: "she is your mother's close relative" (LOGOS) — consistent with v. 12.

**V. 14 — Father's brother / "approach his wife."** EMET: "you shall not approach his wife; she is your aunt." LOGOS: "you shall not approach his wife; she is your aunt." PNEUMA: "Do not dishonor your father's brother by approaching his wife. She is your aunt." Consensus: "you shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's brother; you shall not approach his wife — she is your aunt." The MT at v. 14 is structured differently from adjacent verses: it states "you shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's brother" (as the governing prohibition), then explains it by "you shall not approach his wife." EMET preserves this structure; LOGOS does also. PNEUMA restructures and removes the "uncover nakedness" idiom. Consensus restores the full two-clause MT structure. Note: the semicolon is changed to an em-dash before "she is your aunt" to match the style used in adjacent verses.

**V. 15 — Daughter-in-law.** All three largely agree. EMET: "she is the wife of your son; you shall not uncover her nakedness." LOGOS: "she is your son's wife, you shall not uncover her nakedness." PNEUMA: "She is your son's wife; you must not violate her." Consensus: "she is your son's wife, you shall not uncover her nakedness" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "violate" replaces the idiomatic formulation against the CRITICAL RULING.

**V. 16 — Brother's wife.** EMET: "it is your brother's nakedness." LOGOS: same. PNEUMA: "That would dishonor your brother." Consensus: "it is your brother's nakedness" — the MT's ervat achikha hi is a precise statement that the sister-in-law's nakedness belongs categorically to the brother; PNEUMA's paraphrase loses this legal-relational logic.

**V. 17 — Woman and her daughter / zimmah.** EMET: "they are her near-kin — it is wickedness." LOGOS: "they are close relatives — it is depravity." PNEUMA: "they are her own flesh. That is depravity." Consensus: "they are close relatives — it is depravity." "Close relatives" (LOGOS) for she'ereh hennah is the established chapter rendering. "Depravity" is the ruling term for zimmah (per terminology rulings). EMET's "wickedness" is weaker than zimmah demands; "depravity" captures the calculated vile nature of the act. PNEUMA's "own flesh" for she'ereh is acceptable but inconsistent with the chapter's "close relative" ruling.

**V. 18 — Rival wife / sister.** EMET: "a woman in addition to her sister you shall not take to make her a rival; to uncover her nakedness beside her in her lifetime." LOGOS: "you shall not take a woman as a rival to her sister, uncovering her nakedness while her sister is still alive." PNEUMA: "Do not take your wife's sister as a rival wife and have relations with her while your wife is still living." Consensus: "you shall not take a woman as a rival to her sister, to uncover her nakedness while her sister is still alive." LOGOS's rendering is followed closely. PNEUMA's "your wife's sister" makes the relationship explicit in a direction the Hebrew does not: the MT says ishah el achoteha ("a woman alongside her sister"), not "your wife's sister." PNEUMA also resolves the "uncover nakedness" idiom. EMET's "to make her a rival" is accurate (from tsarar = to be a rival/adversary) but the restructured LOGOS form reads more naturally. The infinitive "to uncover her nakedness" (EMET, adapted) replaces LOGOS's participial form "uncovering her nakedness" — the Hebrew le-gallot ervatah is an infinitive construct and the infinitival rendering better preserves the purposive/resultative sense.

**V. 19 — Menstrual impurity.** EMET: "in her menstrual separation." LOGOS: "while she is in her menstrual impurity." PNEUMA: "during her time of monthly uncleanness." Consensus: "during her menstrual impurity" — niddah is the technical term (established in LEV 012 as "menstrual impurity [niddah]"). EMET's "menstrual separation" is literal (niddah from nadad, "to be separated") but "impurity" is the more standard and established rendering. PNEUMA's "monthly uncleanness" is too casual and loses the precise legal term. The verse uses the "uncover nakedness" idiom: "You shall not approach a woman to uncover her nakedness during her menstrual impurity" — the idiom is preserved per CRITICAL RULING. LOGOS adds "while she is in" which is interpretive elaboration; "during her menstrual impurity" is more compact.

**V. 20 — Neighbor's wife / "lie carnally" vs. "lie sexually."** EMET: "you shall not give your emission to become impure." LOGOS: "you shall not lie sexually with your neighbor's wife and so make yourself unclean with her." PNEUMA: "Do not have sexual intercourse with your neighbor's wife and defile yourself with her." Consensus: "you shall not lie carnally with your neighbor's wife, making yourself unclean with her." The Hebrew v. 20 uses a different verb structure from the "uncover nakedness" idiom: lo-titen shekhovtekha le-zara ("you shall not give your lying to seed" — a euphemism using shikevah, "lying/emission"). EMET is too literal with "give your emission." LOGOS's "lie sexually" is clear. PNEUMA's "sexual intercourse" is clinical. Consensus: "lie carnally" — a formal-equivalent rendering that preserves the sexual and embodied character of the Hebrew without being clinical or colloquial. "Making yourself unclean with her" renders ve-nitmeita bah (Niphal reflexive of tame) — the defilement acts on the actor.

**V. 21 — Molech / "pass through."** EMET: "from your seed you shall not give to pass through to the Molekh." LOGOS: "You shall not give any of your children to pass them through to Molech." PNEUMA: "Do not give any of your children to be passed through the fire as an offering to Molech." Consensus: "You shall not give any of your children to pass them through to Molech." LOGOS's rendering is followed. The Hebrew le-ha'avir la-Molekh is "to pass through to Molech" — the phrase is the established technical term; "pass through" is preserved without specifying the mechanism (fire, consecration, etc.), consistent with the Hebrew idiom. PNEUMA's addition of "through the fire as an offering" goes beyond the MT at this verse (fire is not specified here, though cf. 2 Kgs 23:10); this is an interpretive expansion. EMET's "from your seed" is more literal (mi-zar'akha) than "any of your children" — both are acceptable, but "children" (LOGOS) is clearer and conventional. "And so profane the name of your God" — all three preserve this; consensus adopts LOGOS's rendering. The LXX variant (ἄρχοντι = "to the ruler" rather than Molech) is noted but the MT is followed.

**V. 22 — Male lying / "abomination."** EMET: "with a male you shall not lie as the lyings of a woman — it is an abomination." LOGOS: "You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination." PNEUMA: "Do not lie with a man as one lies with a woman. That is an abomination." Consensus: "You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination." LOGOS's rendering is adopted. All three translate teva'ah hi as "it is an abomination" — the ruling term. EMET's "as the lyings of a woman" (mishkevei ishah, construct plural) is the most literal but slightly awkward. LOGOS's "as with a woman" is idiomatic and accurate. PNEUMA's "a man" vs. EMET/LOGOS "a male" — zachar is the Hebrew sex-term ("male"), not merely "man" (ish); "a male" (EMET/LOGOS) is more precise for the legal text.

**V. 23 — Bestiality / tevel.** EMET: "you shall not give your emission to become impure by it... it is a perversion." LOGOS: "you shall not lie with any animal and so make yourself unclean with it... it is depravity." PNEUMA: "Do not have sexual relations with any animal. Do not defile yourself with it... That is a perversion." Consensus: "And you shall not give your emission to any animal, making yourself unclean with it; nor shall a woman offer herself to an animal to lie with it — it is depravity." The ruling term for tevel is "depravity" (per terminology rulings). EMET's "perversion" and PNEUMA's "perversion" are set aside in favor of "depravity" as the ruling term. EMET's "give your emission" is literal and is retained for the male clause (shekhovtekha for the male side, specifically tied to emission/lying). LOGOS's "lie with any animal" collapses the emission nuance but is cleaner. Consensus adopts a rendering close to EMET's structure for the male clause and LOGOS for the female clause. "Nor shall a woman offer herself to an animal to lie with it" — LOGOS's rendering is adopted; PNEUMA's "offer herself" is the same concept.

**V. 24 — "Make yourselves unclean" / closing frame.** EMET: "You shall not make yourselves impure." LOGOS: "Do not make yourselves unclean." PNEUMA: "Do not defile yourselves." Consensus: "Do not make yourselves unclean" (LOGOS) — "unclean" is the established rendering for tame throughout Leviticus. "Defile" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but "unclean" is the consistent chapter term. The second clause: EMET: "in all these the nations were made impure." LOGOS: "by all these things the nations... have made themselves unclean." PNEUMA: "this is how all the nations... defiled themselves." Consensus: "by all these the nations I am driving out before you have made themselves unclean" (LOGOS, condensed) — the reflexive ("have made themselves") is correct for the Niphal form.

**V. 25 — "Visited its iniquity" vs. "punished its iniquity."** EMET: "I visited its iniquity upon it." LOGOS: "I punished its iniquity." PNEUMA: "I punished it for its wickedness." Consensus: "I visited its iniquity upon it." The Hebrew va-efqod et-avonah aleha uses the verb paqad ("to visit/reckon/punish") with the noun avon ("iniquity"). EMET's "visited its iniquity upon it" is the most literal and preserves the distinctive biblical idiom of paqad al ("to visit upon"). LOGOS's "punished its iniquity" is interpretively accurate but loses the specific Hebrew juridical idiom. PNEUMA's "wickedness" for avon is weaker than the standard "iniquity" rendering. "The land vomited out its inhabitants" — all three preserve the vomiting metaphor; consensus retains it. "Became unclean" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "became defiled" (PNEUMA): consensus uses "became unclean" for consistency with the chapter's tame terminology.

**V. 26 — "Native... sojourner."** EMET: "neither the native nor the sojourner who sojourns." LOGOS: same. PNEUMA: "whether native-born Israelite or foreigner living among you." Consensus: "neither the native nor the sojourner who sojourns among you" (EMET/LOGOS) — ha-ezrach and ha-ger ha-gar betokhekhem. The cognate verbal construction ha-ger ha-gar ("the sojourner who sojourns") is preserved in EMET and LOGOS; PNEUMA's "foreigner living among you" loses this deliberate wordplay. "Native-born Israelite" (PNEUMA) over-specifies — ha-ezrach is simply "the native" (native-born inhabitant of the land).

**V. 27 — "People of the land."** EMET: "the men of the land." LOGOS: "the people of the land." PNEUMA: "the people of the land." Consensus: "the people of the land" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — anshei ha-aretz could be rendered "men of the land" (EMET, literal) but "people of the land" is the natural English equivalent and avoids the gender-specific implication.

**V. 28 — "Shall not vomit you out" / "lest."** EMET: "And the land shall not vomit you out when you make it impure." LOGOS: "lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean." PNEUMA: "it will vomit you out just as it vomited out the nations before you." Consensus: "lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean, as it vomited out the nation that was before you" (LOGOS). The Hebrew uses ve-lo-taqe ha-aretz... ("and the land shall not vomit") with a conditional nuance ("lest it vomit") — LOGOS's "lest" correctly captures the warning-conditional. EMET's "shall not vomit you out when you make it impure" is slightly awkward. PNEUMA restructures as a prediction rather than a warning. "The nation that was before you" (LOGOS) vs. "the nations before you" (PNEUMA): the MT uses ha-goy in the singular; "the nation" is more precise. "Make it unclean" (LOGOS) vs. "make it impure" (EMET): consensus uses "unclean" for consistency.

**V. 29 — "Cut off from among their people."** EMET: "even those persons from the midst of their people" (appears truncated). LOGOS: "the persons who do them shall be cut off from among their people." PNEUMA: "whoever it may be — shall be cut off from their people." Consensus: "those persons shall be cut off from among their people" (LOGOS) — the Hebrew ve-nikhreitu ha-nefashot ha-osot ("and those persons who do them shall be cut off") uses the standard karet formula. "From among their people" preserves the me-qerev amam ("from the midst of their people") phrasing. PNEUMA's "from their people" drops "among"; consensus retains "from among."

**V. 30 — "Abominable customs" / chuqqot ha-tova'ot.** EMET: "the statutes of the abominations." LOGOS: "abominable customs." PNEUMA: "the abominable customs." Consensus: "these abominable customs" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — chuqqot ha-tova'ot is "the statutes/customs of the abominations" or "abominable customs." "Abominable customs" (LOGOS) works as an adjectival compound and maintains the ironic echo of the chapter's opening chuqqot (vv. 3–4, where YHWH commanded Israel not to walk in the chuqqot of Egypt and Canaan). EMET's "statutes of the abominations" is more literal but less idiomatic. "Never to practice" (LOGOS) vs. "to not do from" (EMET) vs. "do not practice" (PNEUMA): consensus adopts "never to practice" (LOGOS) — the lo ta'asu (negated imperfect) with the context of "my charge" carries a permanent prohibition sense. "Do not make yourselves unclean by them" — consensus: "do not make yourselves unclean by them" (LOGOS, consistent with the chapter's tame vocabulary). The final "I am the LORD your God" — all three preserve; consensus retains.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the Egypt-Canaan frame (vv. 1–5, 24–30):** The chapter's literary architecture is chiastic: the opening frame (vv. 1–5) prohibits walking in the chuqqot of Egypt and Canaan and promises life through obedience (va-chai bahem); the closing frame (vv. 24–30) describes the consequence of those same chuqqot as defilement and expulsion. The same word chuqqot appears in both frames, deliberately: v. 30 labels the Canaanite practices as chuqqot ha-tova'ot ("abominable customs"), the ironic counterpart to YHWH's own chuqqot (v. 3). This architectual echo is preserved in the consensus rendering.

**On va-chai bahem (v. 5):** "He shall live by them" is one of the most cited phrases in the entire Torah. Paul quotes it in Gal 3:12 and Rom 10:5 as part of his argument about Torah-obedience versus faith; Ezekiel cites it at 20:11, 13, 21; Nehemiah at 9:29. The LXX renders it ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς ("he shall live in/by them") — the preposition ἐν can mean either "in" (participatory) or "by" (instrumental), which Paul exploits. The consensus follows "by them" (instrumental), consistent with LOGOS and EMET.

**On the "uncover nakedness" idiom (vv. 6–19):** The Hebrew le-gallot ervah (Pi'el of galah + ervah) is a consistent legal euphemism for prohibited sexual intercourse. It appears approximately twenty-four times in this chapter. The consensus preserves it as "uncover nakedness" throughout, per the CRITICAL RULING. This preserves the idiomatic register of the Hebrew text and avoids the over-specification of "have sexual relations" (NIV) or "have intercourse" — the metaphor of uncovering carries its own moral weight (exposure of what should remain veiled, violation of dignity and boundaries) that clinical translations lose.

**On the Molech verse (v. 21):** The placement of the Molech prohibition within the sexual catalogue is deliberate: the misuse of generative sexuality reaches its theological extreme in destroying the life it produces. The LXX Vaticanus reads ἄρχοντι ("to the ruler") rather than Μολόχ, a significant variant likely reflecting early Jewish avoidance of the divine name and/or an alternate vocalization of melek ("king") without the Masoretic shame-vocalization. The consensus follows the MT.

**On tevel (v. 23) vs. zimmah (v. 17):** These are two distinct Hebrew evaluative terms, both rendered "depravity" in the consensus but distinguished by context. Zimmah (v. 17) denotes a deliberately devised vile act — calculated, scheming depravity. Tevel (v. 23, a rare term appearing only here and at 20:12) denotes a violation of created categories, a confusion/mixing of kinds. Both are rendered "depravity" as the ruling term; the distinction is preserved in this note.

**On the land-vomiting metaphor (vv. 25, 28):** Va-taqi ha-aretz (from qi, "to vomit") is preserved viscerally throughout. The land is personified as a moral agent with a digestive response to accumulated defilement: when the moral pollution exceeds what the land can contain, it physically expels its inhabitants. The same warning applies to Israel (v. 28): the same moral standard that ejected the Canaanites will eject Israel if it defiles the land. The LXX softens this to προσώχθισεν ("was disgusted/loathed") — the consensus follows the MT's more forceful image. This metaphor anticipates the exile: when the prophets interpret Assyrian and Babylonian conquest, they read it through exactly this framework.
